text.title: Cost Estimation Examples and Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Worked examples for SaaS MVP phased cost and solopreneur build-vs-buy analysis; common failure modes.

example.note: Five-phase estimate: Discovery 2wks=$8,200; Design 2wks=$6,300; Development 8wks=$48,900; Testing 2wks=$8,300; Launch 1wk=$4,300. Subtotal=$76,000. Add 20% contingency=$15,200. Total=$91,200. Contingency sized at 20% because requirements are partially defined (innovative project band).

rule.statement: For solopreneurs, treat own time at market hourly rate as an explicit cost line; never mark it as "free".
rule.rationale: Opportunity cost is real; 100 hours at $150/hr = $15,000. A SaaS at $99/mo x 12 = $1,188 often wins once the line is visible.

example.note: Build: 100 hours x $150 opportunity cost + $500 tools = $15,500. Buy: $99/mo x 12 + 5hr setup x $150 = $1,938. Decision: Buy unless building creates unique competitive advantage.

antipattern.description: Salary equals total cost — applying no overhead multiplier to labor.
antipattern.reason: Employer taxes, benefits, and facilities add 30-50% above salary. Omitting them produces a baseline that is structurally underfunded from day one.

antipattern.description: Flat contingency percentage applied without a risk register.
antipattern.reason: 15% on a $500K project with three high-probability risks is likely insufficient; 15% on a low-risk maintenance project is padding. Risk-driven reserves calibrate to actual exposure.

antipattern.description: Fixed price contract with variable scope.
antipattern.reason: Any scope addition without a corresponding price increase produces silent budget erosion that neither party acknowledges until it becomes a dispute.

antipattern.description: Single-point estimates with no uncertainty range.
antipattern.reason: Empirically, software estimates are systematically optimistic by 50-100%. Without a pessimistic case, the cost baseline has no resilience to the most-likely outcome.

antipattern.description: Re-estimating but not re-baselining after a scope change.
antipattern.reason: The original baseline remains the reference; EVM metrics compare against it even though scope changed, making all variance numbers meaningless.

see-also.link: Tracking cost performance against the baseline using CPI/SPI
Managing scope changes that trigger baseline updates
